NJ School Data Almanac
New Hanover Township
BURLINGTON COUNTY  ·  TRADITIONAL PUBLIC DISTRICT  ·  DFG B  ·  219 STUDENTS (2026)

Ten things the data says about New Hanover Township. Every figure is computed from New Jersey Department of Education data via the njschooldata package — nothing is estimated. Comparisons use District Factor Group (DFG) peers (67 districts).

No. 1 ENROLLMENT

New Hanover’s Enrollment Jumped 18% in a Single Year, Hitting a 12-Year High of 219

After years of bouncing between 166 and 187 students, the tiny Burlington County K-8 district added 34 children in 2026 to reach its largest count on record.

For most of the past decade, New Hanover Township ran a small, slowly shrinking school system. Enrollment slid from 183 students in 2015 to a low of 166 in 2021, the depth of the pandemic years. Then it began to climb back, and in 2026 it leaped: 219 students, up 34 from the prior year, an 18.4% jump in a single fall.

That one-year surge is the largest the district has seen in the available record, and it pushed total enrollment to its peak. Compared with 2015, the district now serves 36 more students, a 19.7% gain over the period. In a district this small, near Joint Base McGuire-Dix-Lakehurst and serving a military-connected community, a swing of a few dozen families can reshape an entire grade.

The COVID years tell part of the story: enrollment dipped to 166 in 2021 before recovering, with a net 5.2% gain from 2019 to 2022. But the 2026 jump dwarfs the earlier rebound. What is driving the sudden influx, and can a district built around fewer than 200 students staff and house a class that just grew this fast?

No. 2 DEMOGRAPHICS

Majority of New Hanover Students Now Qualify for Subsidized Lunch, Up From 43% in 2015

The share of students who are economically disadvantaged rose to 52.6% in 2026, the highest on record, with 115 children now qualifying.

Economic need has deepened in New Hanover Township over the past decade. In 2015, 79 students, or 43.2%, qualified for free or reduced-price lunch. By 2026, that figure had climbed to 115 students, or 52.6%, meaning a majority of the district’s children now come from families that meet the income threshold for subsidized meals.

The jump was not gradual. The share crossed 50% for the first time in 2020, at 58.2%, during the economic shock of the pandemic’s first year, then settled into the low 50s. The 2026 count of 115 is the most students ever enrolled in the program in the district’s record, rising alongside the year’s enrollment surge.

That a small, military-adjacent district has tipped to majority economically disadvantaged matters for how it budgets meals, staffing, and academic support. As enrollment and need both climb, will the district’s per-pupil resources keep pace with the families now walking through its doors?

No. 3 ATTENDANCE

Nearly 1 in 5 New Hanover Students Were Chronically Absent in 2024, Almost Double the Pre-COVID Rate

Chronic absenteeism reached 19.2% in 2024, up from 10.3% in 2019 and edging just above the district’s DFG B peer median.

Before the pandemic, New Hanover Township kept most of its students in their seats: 10.3% were chronically absent in 2019, meaning they missed at least 10% of the school year. By 2024, that rate had nearly doubled to 19.2%, leaving roughly one in five students at risk of falling behind from missed instruction alone.

The spike was sharpest right after schools reopened. Chronic absenteeism climbed to 14.3% in 2021 and peaked at 20.1% in 2022 before easing slightly to 17.1% in 2023, then rising again to 19.2%. The 2024 figure sits just above the median of 18.3% for districts in New Hanover’s District Factor Group B peer group, so the district is not an outlier among similar communities, but it has not returned to its own pre-COVID baseline either.

For a K-8 district where early attendance habits set the foundation for later years, the gap between 10.3% and 19.2% represents a meaningful share of young students missing weeks of school. What will it take to pull the rate back toward where it stood before 2020?

No. 4 DEMOGRAPHICS

New Hanover’s Black Enrollment Share Fell From 19.7% to 14.6% as White Share Rose to 42.5%

Since 2015 the district’s largest demographic shift has been a 5.1-point decline in its Black share, while Hispanic students remain the largest group at 39.3%.

New Hanover Township’s student body has reshaped itself over the past decade. The Black share of enrollment fell from 19.7% in 2015 to 14.6% in 2026, a drop of 5.1 percentage points and the largest single demographic change in the district. Over the same period the white share rose from 37.7% to 42.5%, a gain of 4.8 points, and the Hispanic share edged up 2.1 points to 39.3%.

Hispanic students have remained the district’s largest or co-largest group throughout, never dipping below 35%. The white share, by contrast, has grown steadily in recent years, climbing from 35.2% in 2022 to 42.5% in 2026 to become the plurality.

One quieter shift sits in the multiracial category, which swelled to 12% of students in 2021 before falling back to 3.2% by 2026, a reminder that in a district of barely 200 children, a handful of families can move the percentages sharply. As the district’s enrollment surges, will these trends hold or reverse with the newest arrivals?

No. 5 FINANCE

New Hanover’s Spending Rank Among 67 Peers Climbed From 64th to 23rd as Per-Pupil Costs Fell

Per-pupil spending dropped from a 2019 peak of $22,655 to $19,502 in 2024, yet the district moved up the DFG B spending ranks as peers’ costs rose faster.

New Hanover Township spent $19,502 per pupil in 2024, placing it at the 71st percentile among its 67 District Factor Group B peers, above the peer median of $18,001. But the more telling story is the trajectory. The district’s per-pupil spending actually fell from a 2019 peak of $22,655 to a low of $18,344 in 2023 before ticking back up.

Even as its own spending declined, New Hanover climbed the relative ranks: from 64th among peers in 2014 to 23rd in 2024. In other words, the district grew comparatively more expensive not by spending more, but by cutting less than the communities around it. Since 2014, its per-pupil figure rose just 2.4%, a modest increase over a decade of inflation.

Of every dollar spent in 2025, 59.7% went to the classroom, with 18.6% to support services, 11.9% to plant operations, and 8.2% to administration. As enrollment surges and economic need rises, will the district’s spending follow the students, or will fixed costs in a very small system stretch each dollar thinner?

No. 6 DEMOGRAPHICS

New Hanover’s English-Learner Share Fell by More Than Half, From 21.4% in 2019 to 6.8% in 2026

The number of students classified as English learners dropped from a peak of 38 to just 15, even as overall enrollment hit a record high.

As New Hanover Township’s enrollment climbed to a record 219 students, one population moved in the opposite direction. The share of students classified as English learners, or limited English proficient, fell from a peak of 21.4% in 2019, when 38 children were enrolled, to 6.8% in 2026, when just 15 were. That is a drop of more than half in both share and raw count.

The decline has been steady since 2019, with the English-learner share sliding through 13.2% in 2022, 11.6% in 2023, and 9.2% in 2025 before reaching its current low. The trend runs counter to the district’s growing Hispanic enrollment, which remains its largest group at 39.3%, suggesting that more Spanish-speaking families may be arriving already proficient in English or that students are exiting language services more quickly.

Fewer English learners can ease classroom demands, but it also reflects who is and is not enrolling. In a district adding students fast, will the newest arrivals reverse this decade-long slide, or has New Hanover’s language-services population settled at a new, lower baseline?
